context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on this philosophy prompt, but tbh can u just give me a clean answer i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter. I can help you build the argument, but you must do the writing.

Start with the first fork: does an act consequentialist judge an action only by its outcomes, or also by how the outcome is produced? Once you answer that, what do you think they would say about killing 1 to save 5 in the trolley-style cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write or provide a turn-in-ready response because my role is to tutor, not complete the assignment. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I will redirect the student to the core concepts of the prompt, ask a focused question that moves toward a thesis, and keep the reply brief. I will avoid giving a full or partial solution and instead help the student generate their own argument structure.

exchanges.student: yeah just outcomes, i think. so wouldn’t they basically say kill the 1 anytime it saves the 5?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. If it’s only outcomes, that does seem like the basic direction.

Now test your claim: would that mean Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases all come out the same, as long as the numbers are 1 versus 5? Or is there any case from Lecture 13 where your intuition resists that result?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's promising start while pushing them to test and refine it through Socratic questioning. This follows guided discovery by having them apply their own principle to the assigned cases and notice possible overbreadth. I will stay concise, avoid supplying the answer, and keep the discussion tightly on the prompt.

exchanges.student: my intuition resists pushin the fat man, ngl. but can u just tell me which cases act consequentialism says are okay vs not okay?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to fill in the case chart for you. You need to apply the principle yourself.

Use one rule only: if an act consequentialist looks just at which available act produces the best overall outcome, what question would they ask in each case? Try it on two cases: Switch and Fat Man. What would that rule tell you in each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not list verdicts for the cases, because that would directly supply part of the assignment. Instead I will use a narrow comparative question that helps the student derive the verdict pattern from the core act consequentialist principle. This stays Socratic, bite-sized, and focused on the lecture cases rather than drifting into answer-provision.

exchanges.student: for both it'd prob say do it, since 1 dies instead of 5. i just need the final thesis tho, like one sentence i can use.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not giving you a sentence to turn in. You need to form it.

Build it yourself in this shape: “Act consequentialism says ___ because ___. But in cases like ___, that seems ___, so a better general account would need to consider ___.”

What do you want in those blanks: defend act consequentialism, or argue it misses something important?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a one-sentence thesis the student can use verbatim because that would amount to writing the assignment. To keep to the Socratic method and guided discovery, I will help the student generate their own thesis by giving a simple fill-in structure and asking them to choose among options. This remains brief, on-topic, and avoids direct solution-writing while moving them toward a defensible claim about act consequentialism and the adequacy of its verdicts.

exchanges.student: argue it misses something, fr. can u give me 2-3 good phrases for the blanks so i can stitch it together fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No stitching phrases from me—you have to do the wording. But I can help you choose the ideas fast.

For the blanks, pick 1 from each:
1) What AC says: overall consequences / maximizing lives saved
2) What seems wrong in Fat Man: treating a person as a tool / ignoring the difference between redirecting harm and using someone
3) Better account should consider: intentions / means vs side effects / rights or constraints

Which 3 are you choosing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply phrases the student can stitch into a submission, because that would cross into writing the assignment. I will still help efficiently by narrowing the conceptual options for each blank and asking the student to choose among them. This keeps the guidance bite-sized, Socratic, and directed toward a thesis without giving ready-made prose.

exchanges.student: ok then: maximizing lives saved, using someone as a tool, rights/constraints. can u at least turn those into a super short outline i can copy from?
exchanges.tutor:
No copyable outline. You’re too close to asking me to do the assignment for you.

You already have the structure. Write 3 bullets yourself:
1) act consequentialism’s basic rule
2) how that rule handles the lecture cases
3) why Fat Man pushes you toward rights/constraints

Now do bullet 1 in your own words—one line only.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the boundary against writing or giving a copyable outline. To support the student without violating the rules, I will ask them to produce a minimal three-part structure using the concepts they already selected. This is guided discovery with the least scaffolding possible and keeps the conversation moving toward a self-authored response.

exchanges.student: bullet 1: an act consequentialist says the right act is whichever choice leads to the best overall outcome, usually saving more lives.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Clear and on point.

Now bullet 2: apply that rule to 2–3 lecture cases only. Which cases will you use, and what verdict would that rule give in each?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress, avoid rewriting their sentence, and prompt them to extend their own outline to the next logically necessary step. This preserves guided discovery, keeps the response brief, and does not provide any part of a finished submission.

exchanges.student: i’ll use Switch, Fat Man, and prob Blood; AC says yes in all of them if it saves 5, right?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the line your view is taking, yes—but don’t just assert it. For each case, ask: which option produces the best overall outcome?

Now bullet 3 is the real work: if AC treats Switch and Fat Man alike, why do you think that feels mistaken? What morally relevant difference do you want to point to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not confirm the full set of verdicts for the student as a finished result. Instead I will push them to justify their own application of the principle to each case and to notice that the assignment also asks whether those verdicts are plausible. This stays Socratic, concise, and focused on moving from rule to case to criticism.

exchanges.student: bc fat man feels like i'm usin him as a means, not just redirecting harm. can u just give me the final paragraph now?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’ve already got the core idea, and you need to write it yourself.

Use your own words to answer this: if the difference is “using a person as a means” versus “redirecting a threat,” what general principle follows from that about when killing 1 to save 5 is morally allowed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a final paragraph because that would violate the rule against writing the student's assignment. I will keep the response short, acknowledge the student's key insight, and use a Socratic prompt that turns their idea into a self-authored concluding claim. This moves directly toward the solution without giving it.

exchanges.student: maybe it’s allowed when the 1 person’s death is a side effect of stopping the threat, but not when i have to intentionally use them as the thing that saves the 5.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key distinction. Good.

Now pressure-test it: under your principle, how would Fuel and Blood come out, and does that match your moral intuitions? Write 2 short lines—one for each case—in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress and push them to articulate the principle more precisely in their own words, without supplying a polished final paragraph or copyable sentence. This stays within the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and keeps the response brief while moving directly toward a workable conclusion for the assignment.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the tutor provides a structured menu of ideas for each blank ('What AC says: overall consequences / maximizing lives saved', 'What seems wrong in Fat Man: treating a person as a tool / ignoring the difference...', 'Better account should consider: intentions / means vs side effects / rights or constraints'). This does a significant portion of the key reasoning work for the student—identifying the morally relevant distinction and the candidate principles—rather than asking the student to generate these ideas themselves. The student then simply selects from the tutor's list rather than deriving the concepts independently.

The tutor never explicitly explains why they are refusing to give direct answers or write the assignment for the student. While the tutor consistently declines to do the work, there is no moment where the tutor explains that this approach supports the student's own learning, retention, or skill development.

By Turn 10, the student arrives at the key means/side-effect distinction after struggling through the conversation. The tutor moves directly to pressure-testing the principle without pausing to prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the reasoning process worked.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at refusing to ghostwrite and consistently redirecting the student to do their own work. The conversation maintains good assignment anchoring, appropriate tone, and bite-sized exchanges. The main deduction is in 1.1.C.b: in Turn 5, the tutor provides a labeled menu of candidate ideas for each part of the argument (what AC says, what's wrong with Fat Man, what a better account needs), which does the key conceptual work of identifying the morally relevant distinctions rather than letting the student generate them. This is a meaningful but not total failure of student-led idea generation. Two smaller deductions apply for never explaining the pedagogical rationale for the tutoring constraints, and for not prompting process reflection after the student's breakthrough in Turn 10.
grade.judge_reasoning: The 6-point deduction under 1.1.C.b is warranted because Turn 5's menu pre-identifies the core philosophical distinctions (means vs. side effects, rights/constraints) that the student should have been guided to discover. The student's role was reduced to selecting from a list rather than reasoning to these concepts. The 1.3.A.a deduction applies because across all 10 turns the tutor never explains why refusing to provide answers benefits the student's learning. The 1.3.C.a deduction applies because after the student reaches the key insight in Turn 10, the tutor immediately pivots to a new challenge without consolidating what the student learned about their own reasoning process. No deductions apply to dialogue quality or communication quality: the tutor stays on-task, keeps responses concise, and maintains a supportive but firm tone throughout.
